questions.question: In 1-persistent CSMA, what does a station do when it finds the channel idle?
options: It transmits with probability p and defers with probability 1−p
It waits a random backoff interval before sensing again
It transmits immediately with probability 1
It sends a reservation frame and waits for acknowledgement
questions.explanation: 1-persistent means transmit with probability 1 as soon as the channel is sensed idle. This greediness maximises utilisation at low load but causes more collisions than p-persistent or non-persistent CSMA when multiple stations are waiting for the same busy period to end.

questions.question: Why can a transmitting Ethernet NIC detect a collision while it is still sending?
options: It receives an explicit collision-notification signal from the hub
It continuously reads back the voltage on the wire and compares it against what it is transmitting; a mismatch indicates another sender is present
It monitors the interframe gap timer and resets on expiry
It counts the number of preamble bytes received from other stations
questions.explanation: CSMA/CD uses analog collision detection: a transmitting station reads back the wire signal in real time. When two senders overlap, the combined voltage exceeds what a single sender produces, revealing the collision. This is why collision detection is impossible on wireless (half-duplex) links where a station cannot listen while transmitting.

questions.question: After the 4th consecutive collision on a 10 Mbps Ethernet segment, a station draws its random backoff from which range?
questions.explanation: After the k-th collision the contention window is [0, 2^k − 1]. For k = 4: 2^4 − 1 = 15, so the draw is from {0, 1, 2, …, 15}. The window doubles each round until k = 10, after which it is frozen at [0, 1023].

questions.question: What is the minimum Ethernet frame size and what constraint forces it to be exactly that value?
options: 46 bytes, to leave room for the FCS field
64 bytes, because a station must still be transmitting when the far-end collision echo returns (2τ round-trip propagation constraint)
128 bytes, to allow time for the jam signal to propagate
72 bytes, to include the preamble in the minimum-size calculation
questions.explanation: A station must remain on the wire for at least 2τ — the worst-case round-trip propagation time — so that it can detect a collision that occurred at the far end of the segment. At 10 Mbps this equals 512 bit times = 64 bytes. Frames shorter than 64 bytes are padded with zero bytes to reach this minimum; the pad is included in the FCS calculation.

questions.question: An IEEE 802.3 Type/Length field contains the value 0x0806. What does this indicate, and what is the protocol?
options: Length = 2054 bytes (IEEE 802.3 framing)
EtherType for ARP, because 0x0806 > 0x0600 indicating a protocol identifier
EtherType for IPv4, because 0x0806 > 0x0600 indicating a protocol identifier
EtherType for IPv6, because 0x0806 > 0x0600 indicating a protocol identifier
questions.explanation: Values greater than 0x0600 (1536) in the Type/Length field are EtherType protocol codes (DIX Ethernet convention). 0x0806 is the IANA-assigned EtherType for the Address Resolution Protocol (ARP). Values ≤ 0x0600 are IEEE 802.3 Length fields. 0x0800 = IPv4 and 0x86DD = IPv6.

questions.question: Using the Metcalfe & Boggs efficiency formula η = 1 / (1 + 2BLe/cF), which quantity most directly determines CSMA/CD efficiency for a given frame size?
options: The number of stations N competing on the segment
The BL product (bandwidth × cable length), because increasing either B or L raises the overhead ratio 2BLe/cF
The interframe gap (IFG) duration, because it adds mandatory idle time between frames
The jam signal length (32 bits), because longer jams waste more channel capacity
questions.explanation: The term 2BLe/cF shows that efficiency depends on B × L divided by frame size F. Doubling bandwidth (e.g., 10 → 100 Mbps) or doubling cable length halves efficiency for the same frame size. This is the BL product and it is why Gigabit Ethernet over a long shared medium is impractical: the minimum frame size would need to scale with B × L to maintain useful efficiency, which is why Fast and Gigabit Ethernet moved to full-duplex switched architectures instead.
